speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 22, 1996. The survivor is Eva Weiner, W-E-I-N-E-R, born Sosna, S-O-S-N-A. Interviewer is Gloria May, G-L-O-R-I-A M-A-Y. We're in Plantation, Florida. The country is the USA and the language is English. Today is October 22, 1996. I'm here to interview Eva Weiner born Sosna. My name is Gloria May. We're in Plantation, Florida, USA.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is tape one of the interview of Eva Weiner. Thank you very much for being with us. We'll begin if you can please tell me your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Eva Weiner and in Poland was Chawa Weiner, Chawa Weiner.

speaker: interviewer
text: What name were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born Chawa-- Chawa Sosna, and when I became a citizen, I changed my name to Eva Weiner. And then you have to know and when I'm born 1916, May the 25th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your family? What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Abram. My mother's name was Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had three brothers and four sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: The older sisters was Rivka. The second was [? Wilba. ?] And the third was Sonia. And the fourth, I am Chawa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, the oldest one was Arzek, Shlomo, and Dawid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how big was Rowne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rowne had 100,000 Jews. It was a big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what percentage of the population was that, the Jewish population, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish 100,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how big was Rowne altogether, in addition to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a big city, a very big city. I couldn't remember how many hundred thousand people, but it was a big city, a known city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Jewish life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very interesting, very good till they started-- the-- the first the Polish people started to attack the Jews. And then when Hitler came that was the end of the Jewish life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember any incidents of the anti-Semitism before 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it started like the Russian came to Rowne in 1939, and it was like two years. But before the Russian came, the Polish people started to attack the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1940, 1938, '39 within these years.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, when you were a child. Do you remember any anti-Semitic acts at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was just-- I think Poland was so wonderful. That's for us. I don't remember beating up. I don't remember killing. I don't remember anti-Semitism. Nothing of that kind till the Poland started, and then the Ukraine started to attack Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite time as a child? What made you happiest?

speaker: interviewee
text: What make me happy? I was going to school. I went to Tarbut. I went to [INAUDIBLE]. I went-- I was [NON-ENGLISH]. I was [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know how to say in Polish. How you say in English? [NON-ENGLISH]-- kibbutz. I had 150 young kids, and I was in [INAUDIBLE] sending them to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you planning on going to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, just before the war started my papers was-- came, and I had to go, but I want to send more people away. So I postponed my going and then the war started and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you supposed to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I supposed to leave in 1946-- '36.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was-- were other members of your family going to go also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they was against it because my father was a rabbi. And I was the one, I went out of-- of these things. I was very much for Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- you father was what kind of rabbi? Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orthodox. What was his synagogue like? Well, it was-- he-- we had a lot of synagogues but where my father belonged was a small synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard for you to be the rabbi's daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: In some ways, yes. Like I went to the kibbutz. My father didn't [INAUDIBLE]. He didn't like it, because it wasn't his way to raise the children. And of course we have to be [INAUDIBLE], kosher. I'm still kosher. I still keep a kosher house. And I couldn't do like other girls and boys was gonna go where they want to. So in that respect, we respected our father very much. And it's European children are-- the-- pay attention to their parents, not like in other countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any other kind of business that your father had besides being a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father didn't make a good living, so my oldest brother was doing side business. I don't remember even what he was doing, but he was the supporter of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you marry before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you get married?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, we was lovers from school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- about how old were you when you met him?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I met my husband I was about 16 or 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meyer Weiner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the family like his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very much. He was a [NON-ENGLISH]. What you say in English? I don't know but [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your wedding.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wedding in Europe was a simple wedding, of course. How shall I describe? Was a chuppah with cake and wine and catering. I don't remember what we had, but it was a nice wedding. Not a big wedding but a nice wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father perform the ceremony?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was another rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he couldn't do it because he was too emotional, upset to give his baby-- his daughter away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you hearing about what was happening in Germany at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, when the Russian war came into Poland, we was thinking that's it. Russia is gonna be [? dead. ?] But we heard rumors that Hitler is trying to come to Europe. But exactly, if I knew exactly, if I remember exactly, I can't say it. It's so many years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if you thought of leaving at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we was only running away from Poland when it started-- when we started to move in. So we were trying to run to Russia, but I got sick and my daughter was three years old. So-- and we couldn't-- we couldn't run away. We didn't have an opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister was living in a small town. So she came with her horses and wagon, or what they call them, and she took me and my daughter with-- When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to her country. That was, I-- I don't remember what year exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's back up a little bit more. Tell me how your husband made a living.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was a decorator, the-- a painter. He used to make those designs on the walls. And that's the way he make the living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you move into your own place with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had our own house. [? As a matter of fact, ?] my husband had a couple houses. We had tenants in the house and everything. But we make the living when we got married. That was when he was single. We're at a grocery store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in the store as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I and my husband was working the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your customers Jewish and non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born in 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rochelle. She's named after my mother Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother had already passed away by that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother passed away when we was young kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would you say your fondest memory was before the war, before the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean in-- at the home?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: The best, my father used to make a Shabbat. I'll never forget that, [SOBBING] never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In which way you want to know? The tables, the-- the love from the parents? They say in the-- from the-- from the books we used to make kiddush and put-- and singing with my father at the table all of the religious songs from the-- from the Bibles. And of course we had the beautiful dinners, [INAUDIBLE] with everything. And we was not allowed to play outside because rabbi's children. That would be a disgrace. But we had a wonderful life, really till Hitler came. We had a wonderful-- I can't, there's not enough time to explain those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you were married did you go back to your parents' house for Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we used to be invited, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your husband religious as well, observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very much. A matter of fact, he danced in [INAUDIBLE], yes. But you know after the war everything changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what happened, then, when the war began.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the war began, when Hitler came, what he did in my city in Rowne, the first day when he came he took 500 men, Jewish men, and we had the jail past the city. And he said he's taking them to work and he killed them all, the first morning when we-- when he came to Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I guess it was in 1939 or '38, something-- something, like that. I really can't recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at that time? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was home. We all was home. It was-- we didn't know what he's gonna do. We think Hitler comes, Germany. We didn't know it's Hitler, he's coming, and he's going to make just as good as Poland, just as good as Russia. We didn't understood what is gonna kill Jews. Can I scratch my nose?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know what happened to the men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, right away the people saw what was happened. The women was free. And then later on, the women would have children on their hands. They was OK, the young girls. The German young fellas, old fellas, I don't know was raping them, was kill-- starting to kill one by one. They raped them, and then they shot them to death. And then they make their parents to stay and watch what they doing to their children. It-- it seemed unbearable to talk about these things. [CRYING] And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he make put us in yellow patches we was wear. And then they make the ghetto for us. And then they make rations-- rations for food. And then what they did [SOBBING] before I went to ghetto, my girlfriend was pregnant. I walked with her. [CRYING] I don't want to talk about it. The German was working, and they had their rifles with that sticks. And he said to her, you Communist. And he killed her with the baby [INAUDIBLE]. I can't talk about these things. [CRYING] And then before the ghetto, they took 6-- 700 children what they could work. And they put a sign on them. And they put a bottle of milk and a piece of bread, and they drove them over the city, and they should scream and holler, Stalin gave us bread! Stalin gave us food! And then they took them and they put them a life in the graves. [CRYING] It's unbearable. You can't talk about those things. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians were there first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the time when the Russians were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they was-- what-- we wasn't used-- we was used to a luxury life in Poland. When they came, everything was in line. You have to go shopping as-- in a line and food was very hard, but it was a life. They didn't kill nobody. They do nothing to-- to the Jews except [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So really and truly, I had to close my store, because there was no business. Communists have [INAUDIBLE] business. There was a [INAUDIBLE]. So I have to close the store, but we had enough food. We had enough. We wasn't starving, let's put it this way. Was your husband still able to do his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody was doing nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was working in-- [? by-- ?] the Russian in a warehouse for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you offered Russian citizenship?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we was Polish, Polish citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the treatment of the Jews any differently at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Russian? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they require you to speak Russian or learn Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened that the Russians left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hitler came, and then that was the end of our life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, what they did-- the first thing was start to hide the husbands, the men, because they was killing the men. They were saying they-- they Communist, they-- they fight for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a barn in the back. And often the family, my father-in-law, my brother-in-laws, my husband, all those, they-- they was in the barn hiding. And I was the provider to cook and bring-- bring them the food and everything. And how about your daughter? What were you doing with your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was in my hands. She was my [? saver. ?] [? It was not right. ?] She was three years old, so where I went I was carrying my daughter with me. And then Rosh Hashanah, when we had-- you know, have to daven. You know what a Torah is. So I went to the synagogue, and I took out the Torah and I took the [? Ukraine ?] shawl. And so-- and I covered like a child and I brought into the barn. And the man was there praying, davening, praying to God. God should save us. That was that, but my daughter never came off my lap, my hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was while the Germans were there, the Rosh Hashanah? What year was that? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember really if it was '39 or if it was '38 or '40. I don't-- you know at that time, you didn't think about the years and about the dates and about the months. Who knew what. We didn't have a calendar. We didn't have nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about trying to leave at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we couldn't run away from the ghetto. It was impossible. What happened to me, really, I looked like a Polish shiksa. So in the ghetto, I was wearing the yellow patches, and I was always wearing on top of my blouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I walked out of the ghetto, I took off the blouse and I carried the blouse on my arm. And this is the way I used to go in to lines where the-- the-- Polish people and get food and bring in food for the ghetto as much as I could. I used to go out three or four times.

speaker: interviewee
text: But once a Polish person recognized me. And he went over to me, and he beat me up terribly, and then he let me go. And soon I was gone, was flying a piece of paper. I can never forget that. He told me to pick it up, so I picked it up. And I said to him-- [? Ritzko ?] [? was his name ?] like today-- I said what you want me to do. He said, I want you to eat it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I don't remember what I did with that until today. I talked sometime to my daughter, and I said what did I do with that paper? Did I eat it? Did I throw away? I was so beaten up, terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then once my brother-in-law, my sister's husband sneaked into my house, and the German came in. And they start to beat him up, and they want to kill him. So I grab my hand I have now, and the bullet went through my right here, my [? palm. ?] And I stopped the-- the bullet. And here somehow he survived. They didn't kill him. I-- I was working in the-- where the soldiers are. So in the morning, we used to go to work. So they undresed us. They showered us with cold water, and we got dressed. And we went to the barracks, they call the barracks, to clean the barracks. That was our doing. And I used to leave my baby with other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you leave her with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With other people, with neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you living with at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter, my husband, and I. We had a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your house was within the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Your house-- my house was demolished when the war, when they was fighting already. The German was going out and the Russian was coming in. It was bombed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. What can I tell you? We was hiding everywhere we could. If it was dead people on the street, we rolled onto the dead people. It was some [? bushes ?] [? between-- ?] it's unbearable to tell and to explain. [SOBBING] I can't explain those things. And that was already Russian bombs and German bombs together. They was fighting. They was going back, the Germans. Or the Russian was coming in. And I was survived by the Russian [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in the ghetto for?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I was in the ghetto a little more than a year, and then I ran away from the ghetto to a [? small. ?] Why did we run away to the-- from the ghetto? I was in the ghetto working. I was giving up numbers for the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a Polish, what I knew of in school came over and told me they gonna have another killing. They gonna take out the whole ghetto, Jews, and they gonna kill them. So if you have an opportunity, and she came from a small town, if you can run away with-- we used to call my daughter Cookie. And your sister and you, her daughter, I'll wait for your people down there. So we tried once and we couldn't. We tried a couple times, and finally we did. We escaped from the ghetto, and-- but four or five weeks after that, the ghetto was liberated, [? taken. ?] How did you try to get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: At night. We got dressed like Polish girls, and we had baskets on our hands with the children. And nobody stopped us, we was walking. And that's the way it was 12 kilometer from Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto enclosed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was closed, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to go out a gate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to pass guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, he was [? around the ?] [INAUDIBLE] I think. We don't-- we didn't go to the city from the ghetto. We went to places where they was giving you food, giving the-- you know, we had cards, food cards and they was giving out. So you have to go and get them. They don't bring it to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your whole family inside the ghetto, your parents and your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But everybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your husband at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the army, in the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: When had he joined the Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't join. They took him when the war started. The-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think the German or the Russian war when they took him to the army. But then by the end he ran away. He was in Russia, then he came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you hearing from him? Were you getting news from him?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not mail, no transport, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long was it that you hadn't heard from him?

speaker: interviewee
text: But then he came. He was hiding with us I guess close to a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about other rules that there were in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no rules. We have no life. We had nothing. It was rules if you have what you eat. You ate if you have what to drink. You drink-- you didn't go nowhere. You couldn't do nothing. It was like-- like animals. We didn't do nothing in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were scared to death. It was a curfew. After 6 o'clock, you can't have lights on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I did with my daughter? Day and night on my lap. I slept with her and ate with her what we had. And then and she [? used to carry on ?] why is the others gone. Why is this, why is that? And then they learn, they learn fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have any other children to play with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we was in the-- in the ghetto you mean? In the ghetto, yes, yeah, the children was gone outside in the daytime. And it was a-- the first time they-- when they took out the first killing was the [INAUDIBLE]. I [? can't-- ?] remember close to 90,000 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop here to change the tape.
